revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime]][[it:Haniyasu]][[ja:ハニヤス]][[pl:Haniyasu]][[zh:波邇夜須毘古神與波邇夜須毘賣神]]
{{short description|Gods of earth, clay, and pottery in Japanese mythology}}
'''Haniyasu-hiko''' and '''Haniyasu-hime''' are two gods of earth, clay, and pottery in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&dq=Haniyasu-hiko&pg=PA47|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|page=47|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Jordan|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aqDC5bwx4_wC&dq=Haniyasuhime&pg=PA111|title=Dictionary of Gods and Goddesses|date=2014-05-14|publisher=Infobase Publishing|page=111|isbn=978-1-4381-0985-5|language=en}}</ref> The two of them are collectively known by the name '''Haniyasu no kami'''. The pair are considered husband and wife as well as siblings. They are also viewed as having the same divine virtues and powers. <ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
The ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'' says that the two of them were born from {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]] after the birth of the great islands of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' says that they were born from {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami's|1=Izanami}} feces, after she died while giving birth to [[Kagu-tsuchi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&dq=Haniyasu-hiko&pg=PA297|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|date=2003|publisher=ABC-CLIO|page=297|isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|language=en}}</ref> <ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />
==Overview==
The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} includes a narrative in which {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}’s feces transform into the deity Haniyasu. During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|qid=Q3545159|lt=Kamiumi|1=Kamiumi}} (*birth of the gods*) by {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} and Izanami, a variety of nature deities are born. When Izanami gives birth to the {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|qid=Q1640658|lt=fire god|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, she suffers severe burns that lead to her death. In her agony before death, Izanami vomits, defecates, and urinates. From her vomit is born the mine deity {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|カナヤマヒコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金山彦神}}, from her feces emerges the clay deity Haniyasu, and from her urine comes the water deity {{ill|Mizuhanome|en|Mizuhanome|qid=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome|1=Mizuhanome}}. This is where the Kojiki ends its account of Haniyasu's origin, with no further description of the deity’s activities.
The ancient Japanese word "Hani" refers to the clay used to create pottery and ceramics, suggesting that Haniyasu is the personification of clay. The birth sequence described in the Kojiki, featuring the fire god, the metal and mineral god, the clay god, the water god, and the food god, symbolizes humanity’s acquisition of metalworking and ceramic technology through fire and the emergence of early agricultural culture, such as slash-and-burn farming. As a result, Haniyasu is also venerated as a guardian deity of pottery and the protector of potters.
Haniyasu is further regarded as a "god of the earth," functioning as a guardian of soil and agriculture. Due to being born from feces, Haniyasu is also considered a fertility deity linked to agricultural fertilization and is sometimes worshiped as a toilet deity. Additionally, Haniyasu is venerated as a protector of construction and landscaping involving soil.
The {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}’s rituals include a different account, where Izanami gives birth to Haniyasu as a fire-calming deity to protect people from the destructive fire god. This interpretation establishes Haniyasu as a "god of fire suppression," and the deity is enshrined in fire protection shrines such as {{ill|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|qid=Q713021|lt=Atago Shrine|1=Atago Shrine (Kyoto)}} and {{ill|Akiba Shrine|ja|秋葉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akiba Shrine|qq=}}. Haniyasu's association with fire suppression is thought to symbolize the use of soil and mud in ancient fire-fighting methods.
===Various Names and Representations===
; Haniyasuhime, Haniyasubime, Haniyasuhimenokami, Haniyasubimenokami * 波邇夜須毘賣神 ({{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}) <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤスビメノカミ"/> * 埴安姫 (*{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|qid=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}*, "Yin-Yang Chronicle") <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤスヒメ"/>
; Haniyasubiko, Haniyasuhikonokami * 波邇夜須毘古神 (*{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}*) <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤスビコノカミ"/> * 埴安彦 (*{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|qid=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}*, "Yin-Yang Chronicle") <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤスビコ"/>
; Haniyasunokami * 埴安神 (*{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Fifth Segment, Sixth Variant) <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤスノカミ"/> * 土安神 ({{ill|Uneoza Taketsuyasugami Shrine|ja|畝尾坐健土安神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneoza Taketsuyasugami Shrine|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture, described in {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}'s *{{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=Kojikiden|1=Kojiki-den}}*) <ref name="古事記伝-五-274"/>
; Haniyamahime, Haniyamabime, Haniyamahimenokami, Haniyamahiminokami * 埴山姫 (*{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Fifth Segment, Second Variant; Fifth Segment, Third Variant; and {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, "Fire-Calming Ritual") <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤマヒメ"/> * 埴山媛 (*{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Fifth Segment, Fourth Variant) * 埴山姫神 (*{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|qid=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}, {{ill|Haruna Shrine|en|Haruna Shrine|qid=Q3128007|lt=Haruna Shrine|1=Haruna Shrine}}) <ref name="日本神名辞典-ハニヤマヒメノカミ"/> * 波尓移麻比弥 (*{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}*) <ref name="日本の神仏-ハニヤマヒミノカミ"/> * 波爾移麻比禰 (*{{ill|Haniyamabime Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比禰神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniyamabime Shrine|qid=Q135504638|1=Haniyamahime Shrine}}*)
; Other Deities Considered Equivalent * Haniudanakami (土生田神) - Enshrined at {{ill|Haniuda Shrine|ja|土生田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniuda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504640|1=Tsuchifutano Shrine}} (Niigata Prefecture, Shikinaisha) <ref name="日本の神仏-ハニウダノカミ"/> * Haniunokami (波尓布神) <ref name="日本の神仏-ハニウノカミ"/> * Haniumeyanokami (埴生女屋神) - Mentioned in the *{{ill|Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|三代実録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sandai Jitsuroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本三代実録|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1052981}}*; enshrined at {{ill|Kamiichinomiya Ōawa Shrine|ja|上一宮大粟神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiichinomiya Ōawa Shrine|qq=}} (Tokushima Prefecture) <ref name="日本の神仏-ハニウメヤノカミ"/> * Taketsuyasunokami (健土安神) - Appears in the *Sandai Jitsuroku* <ref name="日本の神仏-ハニヤスノカミ"/> (See also: [[#Related Figures]])
== Etymology == The name Haniyasu is thought to mean “to knead earth so as to make it soft” or “clay easy”.
<ref name=":0">{{cite web |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=56|title=OLD - Encyclopedia of Shinto - Kami in Classic Texts - Haniyasu|author=Nakayama, Kaoru(中山 郁)|website=Kokugakuin University|date=22 April 2005|access-date=24 November 2020|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201123195437/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=56|archive-date=23 November 2020}}</ref> <ref name=":1">{{cite web |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9411|title=Haniyasu / 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |trans-title=NEW - Encyclopedia of Shinto - Kami in Classic Texts - Haniyasu|author=Nakayama, Kaoru(中山 郁)|website=Kokugakuin University |date=21 October 2021|orig-date=22 April 2005|access-date=21 October 2021|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211019192435/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9411|archive-date=19 October 2021}}</ref>
<ref>{{Cite book|last=Aston|first=W.G. |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DNPOCgAAQBAJ&dq=Haniyasuhime&pg=PT108 |title=Shinto - The Ancient Religion of Japan|date=2015-11-16|chapter=ch.VII-The Pantheon-Nature Deities page 17|publisher=Read Books Ltd|page=108|isbn=978-1-4733-7719-6|language=en}}</ref> == In the Kiki == ==== Kojiki ==== In Japanese mythology, distinguishing between "birth" (生む) and "giving birth" (産む) can be complex. Here, "産む" is used explicitly for mammalian-like birthing, while "生む" covers other forms of creation, including ambiguous cases.
In the ''Kojiki'', following the creation of heaven and earth (''Tenchi Kaibyaku''), the emergence of the ''Separate Heavenly Deities'' (''Bettenjin''), and the ''Seven Generations of Divine Beings'' (''Kamiyo Nanayo''), the narrative shifts to the ''Kuniumi'' (birth of the land) and ''Kamiumi'' (birth of the gods) by Izanagi and Izanami. Izanami, after giving birth to various deities, delivers the fire god, Kagutsuchi. However, during this childbirth, she sustains severe burns to her genitals and passes away as a result.
In her final moments of agony, Izanami vomits, defecates, and urinates, with each of these bodily excretions transforming into deities. For instance, her feces becomes the god Haniyasu, which manifests as a pair: Haniyasubiko and Haniyasubime. These represent the male and female aspects of the deity. ----
=== Original Text from Kojiki === The following original text often contains the annotation "以音" (read as "pronounced as"), indicating that these parts represent phonetic approximations of Japanese words using kanji, rather than retaining the literal meaning of the kanji characters themselves. For example, while "屎成神" literally means "feces becoming a god," the names like "波邇夜須" (Haniyasu) simply use kanji for their phonetic value and not their inherent meaning. ----
==== Selected Modern Translation: ====
# Izanami gave birth to ''Hinoyagihayao no Kami'' (''Fire Swift Male Deity''). # Another name for this deity is ''Hinokakabiko no Kami''. # Yet another name for this deity is ''Hinokagutsuchi no Kami''. # As a result of giving birth to this fire god, Izanami burned her genitals and fell gravely ill. # During her suffering, she vomited, and the vomit transformed into ''Kanayamabiko no Kami'' (''Metal Mountain Male Deity''). # Next, from her vomit, ''Kanayamabime no Kami'' (''Metal Mountain Female Deity'') was born. # Then, as she defecated in pain, her feces became the god ''Haniyasubiko no Kami''. # Following this, another god was born from her feces: ''Haniyasubime no Kami''. # Subsequently, as she urinated, her urine transformed into the deity ''Mitsuhanome no Kami''. # Finally, ''Wakumusubi no Kami'' (''Harmonious Birth Deity'') was born.
The child of this god is called ''Toyoukehime no Kami'' (''Abundant Sustenance Female Deity''). Thus, Izanami, after giving birth to the fire god, departs from the realm of the living. ----
=== Note on Discrepancies in Numbers === Before this section, Izanami also gave birth to ''Tori no Iwakusu no Kami'' (''Bird Rock Ship God'', also known as ''Ame no Torifune'') and ''Ogetsu Hime no Kami''. The ''Kojiki'' text specifies "eight gods" in total, yet enumerating the mentioned deities yields ten:
# Ame no Torifune # Ogetsu Hime # Kagutsuchi # Kanayamabiko # Kanayamabime # Haniyasubiko # Haniyasubime # Mitsuhanome # Wakumusubi # Toyoukehime
This discrepancy has been known for centuries. The scholar Motoori Norinaga suggested counting gendered deity pairs (e.g., Haniyasubiko and Haniyasubime) as a single deity to reconcile the number to eight. However, other parts of the ''Kojiki'' count such pairs separately, making this interpretation inconsistent.
The scholar Hirata Atsutane expanded on Norinaga’s view, noting variations in counting methods based on whether a deity was a division of another (''Wakemitama''). Modern scholars like Nakayama Shūya suggest these inconsistencies arose from multiple compilers contributing to the ''Kojiki'', leading to editorial discrepancies. ==Faith and Worship (信仰)==
{{ill|Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime|en|Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime|qid=Q9285919|lt=Haniyasu|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} is often revered as a deity associated with agriculture<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/><ref name="Wakaru-176"/>. His divine nature encompasses various aspects of agricultural life: the fertile soil that nurtures crops<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>, the mud of rice paddies, fertilizers that enrich the earth<ref name="Visual-064"/>, protective embankments and levees guarding fields from floods<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>, or even sacred ritual implements used in harvest festivals<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>. All these roles identify Haniyasu as a "god of the earth" and a deity of agricultural fertility<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>.
Though Haniyasu is believed to be the personification of clay, the raw material for earthenware and ceramics, clay itself is unsuitable for farming. However, ancient pottery and earthenware were not mere household items but sacred ritual vessels for harvest ceremonies, thus connecting Haniyasu to agricultural rites<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>.
Some texts omit the myth that Haniyasu was born from excrement and describe him simply as an earth deity. Additionally, in the "{{ill|Chinka Norito|ja|鎮火祝詞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinka Norito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" (*Ritual for Fire Calming*), Haniyasu is portrayed as a god who subdues fire, not directly linked to agriculture<ref name="Tōshaku-178"/>.
===God of Soil, Fertility, and Fertilizer (土の神・土壌と肥料の神として)===
Despite variations in the myths between {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, both texts link Haniyasu and the water goddess {{ill|Mitsuhanome|ja|ミツハノメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuhanome|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8191796}} to the emergence of fertility deities such as {{ill|Wakumusubi|en|Wakumusubi|qid=Q10921330|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|1=Wakumusubi}} and {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyoukehime|1=Toyouke-hime}}. In one version of *Nihon Shoki*, Haniyasu directly gives birth to Wakumusubi, the god of grains<ref name="Tōshaku-178"/>. These stories symbolize the vital role of soil and water in producing crops<ref name="Tōshaku-178"/>. Furthermore, the myth of Haniyasu and Mitsuhanome being born from {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}'s excrement connects the agricultural use of manure as fertilizer to their divine roles<ref name="Tōshaku-178"/><ref name="WorldMyth-270"/>.
===God of Embankments and Levees (土の神・畦畔と堤防の神として)===
Soil is essential for building embankments and levees to protect farmland from floods and secure irrigation<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>. In agricultural regions, Haniyasu is worshiped as a guardian deity of these protective structures<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>. Particularly in {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|qid=Q13987|lt=Kyushu|1=Kyushu}}, he is venerated as "{{ill|Haniyamahime|ja|ハニヤマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniyamahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハニヤス|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9285919}}," the guardian goddess of rice paddy embankments<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>.
===God of Pottery and Ceramics (土の神・陶芸の神として)===
{{ill|Suzuki Shigetane|en|Suzuki Shigetane|qid=Q20040844|lt=Suzuki Shigetaka|1=Suzuki Shigetane}} (1812–1863) argued that while *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki* describe Haniyasu as an "earth deity," he should not be considered a god of all earth but specifically of clay, the material for pottery<ref name="Tōshaku-175"/>. In ancient Japanese, "hani" referred to clay for making earthenware, which was shaped and fired to create pottery<ref name="Yomitoki-Haniyasu"/>. The deity Haniyasu is thus seen as the personification of this clay and the ancestral god of pottery and ceramics<ref name="Tōshaku-175"/><ref name="JapaneseDeityDict-HaniyasuHiko"/><ref name="ShintoHistory-HaniyasuHime"/>.
Though clay is unsuitable for farming<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/>, earthenware vessels were central to ancient agricultural rituals, making Haniyasu closely connected to agricultural rites through these sacred tools<ref name="Yomitoki-Haniyasu"/>.
===God of Civil Engineering and Landscaping (土の神・土木工事と造園の神として)===
In modern times, Haniyasu is venerated not only as an agricultural deity but also as a guardian of construction and land development projects. He is worshiped for protection in land reclamation, civil engineering works, and landscaping projects<ref name="Roots-Haniyasu"/><ref name="Wakaru-176"/><ref name="Visual-064"/>.
===Fire-Calming Deity (鎮火の神として)===
At shrines known for their fire prevention blessings, such as {{ill|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|qid=Q713021|lt=Atago Shrine|1=Atago Shrine (Kyoto)}}, {{ill|Akiba Shrine|ja|秋葉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akiba Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Haruna Shrine|en|Haruna Shrine|qid=Q3128007|lt=Haruna Shrine|1=Haruna Shrine}}, Haniyasu is revered as a god who protects against fire hazards<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/>.
===Toilet God (厠の神)===
In the {{ill|Urabe Shinto|ja|卜部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉田神道|1=Yoshida Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1760351}} tradition, {{ill|Haniyamahime|ja|ハニヤマヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniyamahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハニヤス|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9285919}} and {{ill|Mitsuhanome|ja|ミツハノメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuhanome|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8191796}} are worshipped as "*gods of the toilet*" (*Kawaya no Kami*)<ref name="ShogakuKokugo-Kawaya"/>.
==Major Shrines Enshrining Haniyasu (祭神となっている主な神社)==
According to the {{ill|Japanese Society of Soil Science and Plant Nutrition|ja|日本土壌肥料学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Soil Science and Plant Nutrition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (2015) report "*Soil and Eastern and Western Gods, Japanese Land Deities*," there are: - 286 shrines for {{ill|Haniyasuhime|ja|ハニヤスヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniyasuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - 129 shrines for {{ill|Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime|en|Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime|qid=Q9285919|lt=Haniyasu|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} - 24 shrines for {{ill|Haniyasuhiko|ja|ハニヤスヒコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniyasuhiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
Among these, 12 shrines enshrine both Haniyasuhiko and Haniyasuhime together<ref name="SoilGodsV3"/>.
Notably, 100 out of 129 shrines dedicated to Haniyasu are in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}<ref name="SoilGodsV3"/>. *Haniyasuhime* shrines are distributed nationwide, with notable concentrations in Fukuoka, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}<ref name="SoilGodsV3"/>.
===Haniyasunokami Shrines (ハニヤスノカミ)=== * {{ill|Uneoza Takehiyasu Shrine|ja|畝尾坐健土安神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uneoza Takehiyasu Shrine|1=Uneoza Taketsuyasugami Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30931377}} (*Nara Prefecture*, Kashihara City) - {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/> * {{ill|Haruna Shrine|en|Haruna Shrine|qid=Q3128007|lt=Haruna Shrine|1=Haruna Shrine}} (*Gunma Prefecture*, Takasaki City) - {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/> * {{ill|Haniimasahime Shrine|ja|波爾移麻比禰神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniimasahime Shrine|qid=Q135504638|1=Haniyamahime Shrine}} (*Tokushima Prefecture*, Mima City) - {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/> * {{ill|Maoka Shinden Shrine|ja|馬岡新田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maoka Shinden Shrine|qq=}} (*Tokushima Prefecture*, Miyoshi City) - {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/> * {{ill|Haniro Shrine|ja|波爾布神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniro Shrine|1=Hanifu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11555577}} (*Shiga Prefecture*, Takashima City) - {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/> * {{ill|Katahara Shrine|ja|形原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katahara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Aichi Prefecture*, Gamagori City) - {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/> * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Shimada)|ja|大井神社 (島田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Shrine (Shimada)|1=Oi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11432216}} (*Shizuoka Prefecture*, Shimada City)<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniyasu"/>
===Haniyasuhimenokami Shrine (ハニヤスビメノカミ)=== * {{ill|Tsutsuharu Shrine|ja|堤治神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsutsuharu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Aichi Prefecture*, Ichinomiya City)
===Haniyamahime Shrines (ハニヤマヒメ)=== * {{ill|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|qid=Q713021|lt=Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Atago Shrine (Kyoto)}} (*Kyoto City*, Ukyo Ward) – Known for fire protection<ref name="ShrineEngi-Atago"/> * {{ill|Maoka Shinden Shrine|ja|馬岡新田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maoka Shinden Shrine|qq=}} (*Tokushima Prefecture*, Miyoshi City) * {{ill|Haruna Shrine (Fujimi)|ja|榛名神社 (富士見市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haruna Shrine (Fujimi)|1=Haruna Shrine (Fujimi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q128866571}} (*Saitama Prefecture*, Fujimi City) * {{ill|Haniu Shrine (Narita)|ja|埴生神社 (成田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniu Shrine (Narita)|1=Habu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11426350}} (*Chiba Prefecture*, Narita City)
===Haniumeyanokami Shrines (ハニウメヤノカミ)=== * {{ill|Kamiichinomiya Ōawa Shrine|ja|上一宮大粟神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiichinomiya Ōawa Shrine|1=Kamiichinomiya Ōawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11358379}} (*Tokushima Prefecture*, Kamiyama Town) – Haniumeyano-kami (埴生女屋神) is identified with Haniyasu<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniumeya"/>.
===Haniudanokami Shrine (ハニウダノカミ)=== * {{ill|Habuta Shrine|ja|土生田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Habuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504640|1=Tsuchifutano Shrine}} (*Niigata Prefecture*, Tagami Town) – Haniudanokami is regarded as another form of Haniyasu<ref name="JapaneseGods-Haniuda"/>.
